one_line_takeaway: SecureSplit defends Split Learning against backdoor attacks by transforming client embeddings via UMAP compression followed by polynomial-kernel expansion to amplify benign/poisoned separability, then filtering outliers with a variance-adaptive majority-consensus radius around the coordinate-wise median, consistently beating 7 baselines (including the prior SL-specific defense VFLIP) on ACC/ASR across 4 datasets and 5 attacks including a defense-aware adaptive attack.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10 under the VILLAIN attack, SecureSplit achieves ACC 0.85 / ASR 0.06, versus No-defense ACC 0.84 / ASR 0.76, TrMean ACC 0.83 / ASR 0.57, Multi-Krum ACC 0.77 / ASR 0.48, HDBSCAN ACC 0.82 / ASR 0.55, DP ACC 0.83 / ASR 0.57, MP ACC 0.80 / ASR 0.67, ANP ACC 0.82 / ASR 0.48, and VFLIP (the strongest baseline) ACC 0.76 / ASR 0.17. Across all four datasets and all five attacks in Table 1, SecureSplit achieves the highest or near-highest ACC together with the lowest ASR in nearly every cell, e.g. under BadVFL on CIFAR-10 it reaches ACC 0.85 / ASR 0.04 vs. VFLIP's ACC 0.76 / ASR 0.13; under the Adaptive attack (crafted specifically against SecureSplit) it holds ACC 0.87 / ASR 0.08 on CIFAR-10 vs. VFLIP's ACC 0.76 / ASR 0.15. Under increasing poison rate (Fig. 4) SecureSplit keeps ASR below 10% across 0.5%-10% poison rates while MP's ASR exceeds 95% at a 3% poison rate. Under increasing trigger magnitude (Fig. 5) SecureSplit keeps ASR below 0.10 across magnitudes 0.5-8 while DP's ASR approaches 85% under the He attack at magnitude 8.0. With multiple malicious clients (3 of 8, Table 3), SecureSplit again gets the best ACC/ASR across all five attacks (e.g., VILLAIN: ACC 0.86 / ASR 0.04 vs. VFLIP's ACC 0.74 / ASR 0.23). The dimensionality-transformation ablation (Table 4) shows UMAP outperforms PCA, t-SNE, and factor analysis for reduction, and PKT matches or outperforms RBF and generic feature expansion. Computational overhead is reported as only minimal additional cost versus no defense (specific runtime numbers are in Appendix figures not extracted here). SecureSplit is also reported to outperform the U-shaped-SL-specific defense SafeSplit when adapted to that setting (Table 7, in Appendix, not extracted).
core_contribution: The paper proposes SecureSplit, a server-side defense against backdoor attacks in Split Learning (SL, i.e., vertical federated learning with a bottom-model/top-model split). SecureSplit has two steps applied each training round to the set of client embeddings received by the server: (1) an embedding transformation pipeline that first compresses embeddings with UMAP (dimensionality reduction, preserving local geometric structure to keep benign/poisoned embeddings' relative proximity intact) and then re-expands them with Polynomial Kernel Transformation (PKT), which reintroduces nonlinear feature interactions to amplify the separation between benign and poisoned embeddings; and (2) an adaptive majority-based filtering step that computes the coordinate-wise median of the transformed embeddings as a robust center, determines a base radius R that captures at least half of the embeddings (the majority-consensus assumption), and then adaptively enlarges R to R_adp = (1 + 1/(alpha+sigma)) * R based on the variance sigma of the transformed embeddings (smaller variance -> more aggressive radius enlargement to recover benign embeddings; larger variance -> more conservative enlargement to preserve robustness). Embeddings within R_adp of the median are kept as the benign set S used for that training round.
threat_model: Setting: Split Learning where each client holds a disjoint subset of features (vertically partitioned data), computes an embedding via a local bottom model, and sends it to a server that owns the labels and trains a top model on aggregated embeddings, returning gradients to clients. Attacker: controls one or more malicious clients that can poison their local training data or directly craft/manipulate their transmitted embeddings to insert hidden backdoor triggers, causing the top model to misclassify attacker-chosen (triggered) inputs to an attacker-chosen target label at inference time. The attacker is assumed to have access to limited labeled auxiliary data enabling label inference to craft more targeted/stealthy triggers (consistent with the VILLAIN, Fu, BadVFL, and He attacks the paper builds on), and is assumed to remain a minority of the training population (majority of embeddings are benign) to stay covert -- this majority-benign assumption is explicitly the basis for the adaptive filter's design. Defender: the server, which only observes client embeddings (not client data, models, or attack strategy) and must both preserve accuracy on clean inputs and minimize attack success rate on triggered inputs, without prior knowledge of which clients are malicious.
limitations: The paper's own text: the base majority-based filter (radius R) alone tends to misclassify a large fraction of benign embeddings as poisoned, which is why the adaptive radius R_adp correction is needed -- this is presented as a design motivation rather than a fully solved problem, and the paper does not quantify residual false-positive/false-negative rates of the final filter directly (only downstream ACC/ASR are reported). Increasing the reduced dimension d_u is shown to negatively affect performance under the He and Adaptive attacks specifically (Fig. 6), indicating sensitivity to this hyperparameter for certain attacks. The core majority-based filtering relies on an explicit assumption that the majority of clients/embeddings are benign; the paper does not evaluate settings where malicious embeddings approach or exceed 50% of the population. All main experiments use a single malicious client by default (with only one supplementary experiment scaling to 3 of 8 malicious clients on CIFAR-10 only); broader malicious-client-fraction sweeps are not shown. Evaluation is restricted to image classification benchmarks with vertically partitioned feature splits; no non-vision or tabular/vertical-FL-typical (e.g., financial/healthcare) domains are tested despite the paper's own motivating examples being from banking/credit and healthcare. The SafeSplit and U-shaped SL comparison is deferred to an appendix table not extracted in this review pass.

quantitative_results: VILLAIN attack, CIFAR-10: SecureSplit ACC 0.85 / ASR 0.06 vs. No-defense ACC 0.84 / ASR 0.76 vs. VFLIP ACC 0.76 / ASR 0.17
Fu attack, CIFAR-10: SecureSplit ACC 0.85 / ASR 0.05 vs. VFLIP ACC 0.74 / ASR 0.09
BadVFL attack, CIFAR-10: SecureSplit ACC 0.85 / ASR 0.04 vs. VFLIP ACC 0.76 / ASR 0.13
He attack, CIFAR-10: SecureSplit ACC 0.83 / ASR 0.07 vs. VFLIP ACC 0.73 / ASR 0.09
Adaptive attack (crafted against SecureSplit), CIFAR-10: SecureSplit ACC 0.87 / ASR 0.08 vs. VFLIP ACC 0.76 / ASR 0.15
Poison-rate sweep (CIFAR-10, 0.5%-10%): SecureSplit ASR stays below 10% throughout; MP's ASR exceeds 95% once poison rate reaches 3%
Trigger-magnitude sweep (CIFAR-10, 0.5-8, He attack): SecureSplit ASR stays below 0.10; DP's ASR approaches 85% at magnitude 8.0
Multiple malicious clients (3 of 8, CIFAR-10, VILLAIN attack): SecureSplit ACC 0.86 / ASR 0.04 vs. VFLIP ACC 0.74 / ASR 0.23
Dimensionality-method ablation (CIFAR-10, VILLAIN attack): UMAP+PKT (SecureSplit) ACC 0.85 / ASR 0.06 vs. PCA ACC 0.82 / ASR 0.25 vs. t-SNE ACC 0.84 / ASR 0.19
